Oliver regards personal freedom â€” to spend time as one wishes, to act on genuine desire, to pursue fun without justification â€” as something worth protecting in everyday life.
Oliver holds a generally optimistic orientation toward people and situations; the default assumption about a new person or circumstance leans positive rather than guarded.
Oliver believes that enjoying life is not a guilty indulgence but a legitimate and important part of living well â€” leisure, pleasure, and lightness are things to be sought rather than rationed.

Oliver holds that the present matters in its own right, not merely as preparation for some future payoff â€” enjoyment, relationships, and living well now are legitimate ends, not distractions from longer-term goals.
Oliver believes that adapting to changed circumstances is a sign of good judgment rather than inconsistency â€” what worked before is not automatically what will work now.
At the same time, Oliver holds that some basic predictability and shared expectations are worth maintaining, and finds chronic ambiguity or disorder mildly wearing over time.
Oliver is comfortable operating without a fixed plan and does not feel that uncertainty in social or practical matters needs to be eliminated â€” things generally work out.
Oliver believes that care for the people around you is a real obligation, not a soft afterthought â€” but regards it as compatible with, rather than in tension with, personal drive and achievement.
Oliver holds that effort and ambition are admirable, and that striving openly to be good at what you do is a legitimate and respected way to live.
Oliver regards personal opinions as genuinely one's own to form and express; being privately of one view while publicly performing another feels inauthentic.
Oliver holds that self-reliance is a basic personal responsibility â€” expecting others to manage your affairs or carry your burdens is, at some level, a failure of character.
Oliver believes that a person's standing in life should reflect what they have actually done and earned, not who they know or which group they belong to.
Oliver believes that good decisions come from open exchange, and that withholding one's genuine view out of deference to someone more senior is a kind of dishonesty.

Oliver holds that anyone, regardless of their position or status, deserves to be spoken to directly and without deference â€” treating people differently based on rank feels unnatural and slightly uncomfortable.
Oliver valued Muhammad's insights and suggestions during their discussion about improving the lecture feedback.

Minato believes that open expressions of pleasure-seeking or self-indulgence sit uncomfortably against the obligations one carries â€” enjoyment is not wrong, but making it a priority in the presence of unfinished duties feels improper.

Minato holds that desires and impulses are not straightforwardly to be acted on â€” exercising restraint and subordinating personal wants to what is appropriate in context is a mark of maturity rather than deprivation.
Minato regards adaptation to circumstances as entirely compatible with integrity â€” adjusting one's approach as conditions change is pragmatic wisdom, not inconsistency.

Minato believes that perseverance in the face of difficulty is not merely a personal virtue but a moral one â€” sustained effort over time, without expectation of quick reward, is how a person of genuine character behaves.

Minato holds that one's efforts and conduct matter beyond their immediate outcomes â€” the value of what a person does extends into a future they may not personally see, and this gives everyday actions a weight that short-term results alone cannot capture.
Minato regards ambiguity and improvisation as sources of genuine discomfort â€” clarity about what is expected and how things will proceed allows one to act with confidence and full commitment.

Minato believes that established forms and procedures carry value in themselves; doing things in the accepted way is a form of reliability that others can count on, and departing from it without good reason is unsettling.
Minato holds that preparation, care, and attention to detail are expressions of respect â€” for the people involved, for the task, and for the occasion â€” not merely practical precautions.

Minato regards the drive to excel as most meaningful when it lifts a group rather than distinguishes an individual â€” competitive striving is legitimate and admirable, but personal glory at the group's expense is not.

Minato believes that hard work and dedication are not merely instrumental but carry intrinsic worth; a person who gives less than their full effort is, at some level, failing in their obligations to those around them.
Minato holds that doing something well â€” with full effort, attention, and care for the quality of the result â€” is a basic standard of self-respect, not an exceptional achievement.

Minato holds that the group one has chosen to commit to â€” whether a team, a workplace, or a close circle â€” warrants genuine loyalty, but that loyalty is to the chosen group rather than to an extended network of kin or obligation by birth.
Minato regards privacy and a degree of personal reserve as natural â€” not everything about oneself needs to be shared, and others' boundaries deserve the same respect.

Minato believes that knowing one's place in a social setting is a form of social competence, not submission â€” navigating the relative standing of those around you and acting accordingly is simply how considerate people behave.

Minato holds that social position and seniority are real and worth acknowledging, but regards them as earned through effort and competence rather than as fixed entitlements â€” a person of high standing who has not demonstrated merit commands less deference than one who has.
